title: 12. LLM Inference Engineering
subtitle: Roofline Model, KV Cache Evolution, Scheduling & Distributed Serving

sections.heading: Engineering Large Language Model Inference
sections.body: Deploying LLMs in production is a fundamental departure from standard microservice engineering. While a standard REST API is typically bottlenecked by CPU compute or network I/O, LLM serving is defined by the physics of **memory flow through the GPU**. [1] In the modern LLMOps landscape, maintenance involves fundamentally re-engineering how data traverses **high-bandwidth memory (HBM)** and how computational kernels are scheduled across thousands of CUDA cores. [2]

sections.heading: 1. The Performance Physics of Large Language Models

blocks.content: To engineer efficient inference systems, one must first accept that LLMs operate in two distinct physical regimes: the **prefill phase** and the **decode phase**. [3] Standard web application monitoring focuses on 'Response Time,' but in LLM engineering, this aggregate metric obscures the specific hardware bottlenecks that determine user experience. [1]

items.label: Time To First Token (TTFT)
items.detail: Latency between request submission and the first character on screen. A TTFT under 200ms creates the illusion of instantaneity. The bottleneck is the prefill phase, where the model processes the entire user prompt simultaneously to calculate initial context states. [1][4]

items.label: Time Per Output Token (TPOT)
items.detail: Dictates the fluidity of the streaming response. A target of ~30 tokens/sec exceeds human reading speed. The bottleneck is GPU memory bandwidth, as autoregressive generation requires loading billions of weights from VRAM to generate a single token. [1][2]

items.label: Queue Delay
items.detail: Duration a request waits before GPU processing begins. A direct indicator of system saturation. If the KV cache is full, new requests are enqueued until memory is freed, signaling a need for additional GPU provisioning. [1]

blocks.label: TPOT Calculation
blocks.description: Where $T_{total}$ is the total latency and $N_{tokens}$ is the total generated token count. [5]

sections.heading: 1.2 The Roofline Model and Arithmetic Intensity

blocks.content: The engineering 'why' behind these phases is best understood through the **Roofline Model**, which maps an algorithm's performance against hardware limits. [2]

blocks.label: Arithmetic Intensity
blocks.description: The ratio of floating-point operations to memory movement. This determines whether a workload is compute-bound or memory-bound. [11]

blocks.content: In the **prefill phase**, the model performs matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMM) on the entire prompt. For a hidden dimension $d$ and sequence length $s$, the operations scale at $O(s \cdot d^2)$ while memory access scales at $O(d^2)$. For large prompt lengths, the intensity $I$ is high, making the prefill phase **compute-bound**; the GPU's tensor cores are the limiting factor. [3]

Conversely, the **decode phase** performs matrix-vector multiplications (GEMV) to generate one token at a time. The entire weight matrix must be loaded from HBM into the registers for every single token. [2] The $I$ for this operation is approximately **1.0**, far below the hardware's 'ridge point'. [10] Consequently, the GPU cores sit idle while waiting for data to arrive from memory, making the process strictly **memory-bandwidth bound**. [2]

blocks.title: Prefill vs. Decode Summary
blocks.content: **Prefill** = Compute-bound (matrix×matrix, high arithmetic intensity). **Decode** = Memory-bound (matrix×vector, low arithmetic intensity ~1.0). This fundamental duality drives every optimization decision in LLM serving.

To optimize inference, one must deconstruct the transformer's attention mechanism into its memory-intensive components. The state-of-the-art solution to the autoregressive bottleneck is the **Key-Value (KV) Cache**. [15]

During standard inference, token $t$ is calculated by attending to all previous tokens. Naively, this would require recomputing the keys and values for every preceding token at every step, leading to $O(n^2)$ computational complexity. [17] KV caching stores these intermediate activations in GPU memory, reducing the per-token complexity to $O(n)$. [15]

blocks.label: KV Cache Memory Footprint
blocks.tex: M_{KV} = 2 \times L \times d \times s \times B \times 2 \text{ bytes}
blocks.description: For a transformer with $L$ layers, hidden dimension $d$, batch size $B$, and sequence length $s$ using 16-bit precision. The first factor of 2 accounts for Key and Value tensors; the trailing factor represents the 2 bytes required for FP16/BF16 precision. [19]

blocks.title: The Memory Wall
blocks.content: For a Llama-3-70B model with 80 layers and an 8192 hidden dimension, each token requires **2.62 MB** of VRAM. A single 8k context window consumes over **21 GB** of memory per user, creating a 'memory wall' that limits serving capacity. [19]

sections.heading: 2.2 Evolution of Attention: MQA, GQA, and MLA

blocks.content: To combat this linear memory growth, architectural innovations have sought to reduce the number of KV heads. [22]

items.title: Multi-Query Attention (MQA)
items.description: Collapses all query heads into a single shared KV set. Reduces KV cache by a factor of $n_{heads}$.

items.title: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)
items.description: Divides query heads into $G$ groups, each sharing a single KV head. [24]

items.title: Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)
items.description: DeepSeek's innovation: low-rank joint compression of keys and values into a latent space $c_t$. [23]
pros: 80-90% KV cache reduction while maintaining MHA quality. [26]

blocks.label: MLA Latent Compression
blocks.description: By caching the compressed latent vector $c_t$ rather than the full heads, MLA achieves massive memory savings. [26]

blocks.content: LLM engineering requires precise control over how tokens perceive their relative order. [29] Modern transformers like Llama 3 utilize **Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE)**, which rotate query and key vectors in a 2D plane. [29]

blocks.label: RoPE Transformation
blocks.description: For a vector $x$ at position $m$, RoPE applies an orthogonal rotation matrix $R_m$. The beauty of RoPE lies in the attention dot product, where the absolute positions cancel out, leaving only the relative distance. [29]

blocks.content: This geometric interpretation allows for 'infinite' context extrapolation, as rotation is defined for any integer position. [31] However, for engineers, RoPE introduces complexity in KV cache management, as position-aware keys must be stored or re-rotated during retrieval. [33]

Traditional inference engines allocate contiguous memory for the KV cache based on the maximum sequence length. This design is fundamentally inefficient, wasting up to **60-80% of VRAM** through internal and external fragmentation. [35] Modern SOTA engines like vLLM solve this by applying **operating system paging principles** to GPU memory. [35]

blocks.title: PagedAttention Architecture
blocks.code:
graph TD
  subgraph Logical_View
    L0[Block 0]
    L1[Block 1]
  end
  subgraph Block_Table
    T0["Entry 0: Phys 102"]
    T1["Entry 1: Phys 54"]
  end
  subgraph Physical_VRAM
    P54[Block 54]
    P99[Block 99 - Free]
    P102[Block 102]
  end
  L0 --> T0
  L1 --> T1
  T0 --> P102
  T1 --> P54

blocks.content: This virtualization eliminates the need to reserve memory for the 'worst-case' output length. Instead, a new block is only allocated when the current 16-token block is exhausted. [35] This dynamic allocation enables near-zero waste (under 4%) and supports advanced sharing patterns like **prefix caching**, where multiple requests sharing a system prompt point to the same physical memory blocks. [35]
Building a block manager from scratch in PyTorch requires a decoupled architecture that tracks free and allocated physical addresses. [42]

blocks.code:
import torch

class BlockManager:
    def __init__(self, num_blocks, block_size, device="cuda"):
        self.block_size = block_size
        self.num_blocks = num_blocks
        # Pre-allocate physical KV pool:
        self.free_list = list(range(num_blocks))
        self.block_table = {}  # RequestID -> List of BlockIDs

    def can_allocate(self, num_tokens):
        blocks_needed = (num_tokens + self.block_size - 1) // self.block_size
        return len(self.free_list) >= blocks_needed

    def allocate(self, request_id, num_tokens):
        blocks_needed = (num_tokens + self.block_size - 1) // self.block_size
        allocated = [self.free_list.pop(0) for _ in range(blocks_needed)]
        self.block_table[request_id] = allocated
        return allocated

    def append_slot(self, request_id):
        # Triggered when current block is full
        if not self.free_list:
            raise MemoryError("OOM: No free physical blocks")
        new_block = self.free_list.pop(0)
        self.block_table[request_id].append(new_block)

    def free(self, request_id):
        blocks = self.block_table.pop(request_id, [])
        self.free_list.extend(blocks)

blocks.content: The engineering trade-off for PagedAttention is increased kernel complexity. Standard attention kernels assume contiguous memory. To utilize paged blocks, engineers must write custom CUDA or Triton kernels that dereference physical addresses via the block table at runtime. [41] While this adds approximately **10-20% latency overhead** compared to vanilla FlashAttention, the **2-4x improvement in system throughput** justifies the cost. [44]

Static batching is the 'taxi' model of inference: the driver waits for all passengers to arrive and takes them to their destinations together. [1] If one user generates a 5-word response and another generates a 1000-word response, the first user's GPU slot sits idle for 995 steps. [1]
**Continuous batching** (or iteration-level scheduling) transforms this into a 'bus' model, allowing requests to enter and exit the batch at every single token step. [1]

To implement continuous batching, an engineer must solve the problem of uneven sequence lengths within a single tensor. Instead of padding tokens to a fixed length—which wastes quadratic compute—modern engines use **Ragged Tensors**. [15] Incoming prompts are concatenated into a single long sequence. A 2D boolean attention mask is then used to prevent cross-request interference. [15]

The mathematical constraint is that at each iteration $i$, the engine performs exactly one forward pass for every active request. If Request B completes by emitting an <EOS> token, the scheduler immediately replaces its slot with a new request from the queue in iteration $i+1$. [1]

A major challenge in iteration-level scheduling is **Prefill Jitter**. Because a large prefill request requires significantly more compute than a single-token decode, its arrival can cause a spike in Inter-Token Latency (ITL) for all existing users. [5]

Production-grade engines solve this through **Chunked Prefill**. [15] Instead of processing a 2048-token prompt in one pass, the system breaks it into manageable chunks (e.g., 512 tokens). This allows the scheduler to mix prefill tokens with decode tokens in a single batch without exceeding the hardware's compute budget or latency targets. [15]

sections.heading: 5. Security and Alignment: The Shield Model Architecture

blocks.content: Even a perfectly optimized model is a liability without infrastructure-level safeguards. [1] LLM engineers implement **Guardrails** to detect prompt injection, toxic outputs, and personally identifiable information (PII) exfiltration. [55]

blocks.title: The Shield Model Lifecycle (Llama-Guard 3)
items: **Request Ingress**: The user prompt is intercepted.
**Input Classification**: Llama-Guard 3 evaluates the prompt against a 13-category hazard taxonomy (e.g., S1-Violent Crimes, S10-Hate). [59]
**Fast Refusal**: If unsafe, a canned response is returned. The expensive main LLM is never invoked, preserving VRAM and compute credits. [1]
**Generation**: If safe, the main LLM processes the request.
**Output Verification**: The generated text is scanned for hallucinations or policy violations before reaching the client. [1]

blocks.label: DPO Alignment Loss
blocks.description: While guardrails provide runtime protection, alignment during training ensures the model's internal weights prefer safe responses. DPO replaces the Reward Model + PPO loop with a simple classification loss derived from the Bradley-Terry model. [66]

blocks.content: LLM inference is a battle against the 'Memory Wall'. [6] The hardware choice fundamentally dictates the maximum achievable throughput and context length. [19]

blocks.title: NVIDIA A100 vs. H100 Comparative Analysis

blocks.content: For interactive applications, **HBM3 bandwidth** is the most critical factor. An A100 node often struggles with 128k context windows because the bandwidth cannot load the massive KV cache fast enough to maintain interactive latency. [70] The H100, with its 3.35 TB/s bandwidth, is frequently the only viable solution for high-concurrency, long-context serving. [70]

blocks.label: Speed of Light (SoL) Throughput
blocks.description: For a Llama-3-70B model in FP16 (140 GB) on an H100 (3350 GB/s): $T_{min} \approx 41.8$ ms/token. This represents the absolute theoretical minimum latency per token for a single user. [5]

blocks.content: When a model's weights and KV cache exceed the VRAM of a single GPU (e.g., 405B parameters requiring 810GB), engineers must employ parallelization. [19]

items.title: Tensor Parallelism (Strong Scaling)
items.description: Shards internal weight matrices ($W_Q$, $W_K$, $W_V$) across multiple GPUs. Reduces single-user latency by utilizing combined memory bandwidth. [19]
cons: Highly sensitive to interconnect latency; overhead exceeds gains in non-NVLink environments (e.g., PCIe 4.0). [68]

items.title: Pipeline Parallelism (Weak Scaling)
items.description: Places different transformer layers on different GPUs. Easier to implement across nodes with slower networking. [68]
pros: Works across nodes with slower interconnect
cons: Introduces 'bubbles' in the pipeline where GPUs sit idle waiting for activations from previous stages. [68]

blocks.content: Modern engines compose TP and PP to balance latency and throughput. [68]

sections.heading: 8. Implementation Logic: From Scratch to Production

blocks.content: The following code demonstrates a senior AI engineer's approach to wrapping an LLM with iteration-level scheduling and paged memory management. [48]

blocks.code:
import torch
from typing import List, Dict

class ProductionEngine:
    def __init__(self, model_path: str, block_manager: BlockManager):
        self.model = self._load_optimized(model_path)
        self.block_manager = block_manager
        self.active_requests: List = []
        self.waiting_queue = []

    def _load_optimized(self, path):
        # Implementation of FP8 or INT4 quantization loading
        return torch.compile(AutoModel.from_pretrained(path).half().cuda())

    def add_request(self, prompt_tokens: List[int]):
        self.waiting_queue.append({"tokens": prompt_tokens, "generated": []})

    def step(self):
        # 1. Admit new work if memory allows
        while self.waiting_queue and self.block_manager.can_allocate(1):
            req = self.waiting_queue.pop(0)
            self.block_manager.allocate(id(req), len(req["tokens"]))
            self.active_requests.append(req)

        # 2. Prepare Batch Tensors (Ragged Tensor Logic)
        input_ids = self._assemble_ragged_batch(self.active_requests)

        # 3. Model Forward Pass with PagedAttention Hooks
        with torch.inference_mode():
            logits = self.model(input_ids, block_table=self.block_manager.block_table)
            next_tokens = torch.argmax(logits[:, -1, :], dim=-1)

        # 4. Update Request States and Block Manager
        finished = []
        for i, req in enumerate(self.active_requests):
            token = next_tokens[i].item()
            req["generated"].append(token)
            self.block_manager.append_slot(id(req))
            if token == EOS_TOKEN or len(req["generated"]) > MAX_TOKENS:
                finished.append(req)

        # 5. Clean up finished requests
        for req in finished:
            self.block_manager.free(id(req))
            self.active_requests.remove(req)

blocks.content: This architecture ensures that GPU utilization remains high by decoupling request entry from generation length. [47]
In high-throughput environments, the interaction between precision and bandwidth defines the economic viability of the service. [69]

items.label: INT8
items.detail: 50% memory saving. Near-identical quality to FP16. Works on A100/H100. [21]

items.label: FP8
items.detail: Supported natively by H100's Transformer Engine. Provides the performance of INT8 with the dynamic range of floating-point, further reducing quality loss. [72]

items.label: INT4/NF4
items.detail: 75% memory saving. Enables 70B models to run on 24GB consumer GPUs (RTX 4090). Perplexity increases by 1-3%, often imperceptible in chat. [14]

blocks.content: This analysis indicates that even with high-capacity hardware like the MI300X, context length and concurrency are in a direct **zero-sum relationship**. Engineering decisions must be guided by the specific distribution of user sequence lengths. [19]

Maintaining LLMs in production is a constant evolution from rigid batching to fluid, iteration-level resource orchestration. [47] The integration of PagedAttention and continuous batching has increased GPU utilization by over 50% compared to legacy architectures, but the road ahead focuses on **Disaggregated Inference**. [1]

In a disaggregated setup, prefill operations (compute-bound) are separated from decode operations (memory-bound) into different GPU clusters. [54] This allows for independent scaling of compute power and memory bandwidth, ensuring that a single long prompt does not interfere with the streaming latency of thousands of active users. [54]
For the AI research engineer, success in this domain requires a hybrid mastery of transformer mathematics, low-level CUDA optimization, and operating system-level memory virtualization. [2]

points: **Prefill is compute-bound** (GEMM), **Decode is memory-bound** (GEMV). Hardware choice must match the dominant workload profile.
**KV Cache** is the primary memory bottleneck. MLA (DeepSeek) achieves 80-90% reduction over standard MHA.
**PagedAttention** applies OS paging to GPU memory, reducing VRAM waste from 60-80% to under 4%.
**Continuous Batching** replaces static batching, allowing requests to enter/exit the batch at every token step.
**Chunked Prefill** prevents prefill jitter from disrupting active decode streams.
**Disaggregated Inference** (separating prefill and decode to different GPU clusters) is the next frontier for scaling.
Production guardrails require a **Shield Model** (Llama-Guard 3) for both input classification and output verification.
